input: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome (5 Volumes)
output:
The struggle of parties during Pompeius VI's absence was a necessary step in explaining alphabetic development. A single symbol could represent multiple sounds across various languages or even within the same language over time. Thirty-three years had passed since such an introduction was first introduced, yet I still cherish how much the book owed its initial success to Dr. Schmitz's friendly assistance.

I did not take on this task until I had investigated the possibility of someone else being better suited to handle it. When Dr Mommsen's work somehow came into my hands years after its initial appearance, and it revived my interest in studies I had long set aside for more professional pursuits. I had little doubt that its merits would have already attracted sufficient attention among Oxford's learned community, inducing some of her great scholars to translate it into English. It seemed that despite a strong desire to translate it, and several individuals having considered the task, the projects ultimately failed due to various reasons. Mr George Robertson published an excellent translation of the introductory chapters on the early inhabitants of Italy. I wish to acknowledge my obligations to this work, as far as it goes.

Many of our translations from German are so literal that they reproduce the very order of the German sentence. This results in translations that are either unintelligible or far from readable for the English reader. In contrast, others deviate so entirely from the original form that they cease to be true translations in the proper sense of the term. This, however, is a characteristic feature of the original. It owes much of its charm to this aspect. Dr Mommsen often employs expressions not found in dictionaries, taking full advantage of the German language's limitless possibilities for coining new words or combining existing ones. instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 27 to 36 by Livy'
input: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 27 to 36 by Livy
output:
In Italy, Consul Marcellus regained control of Salapia, which had been surrendered to him. He then captured Maronea and Meles from the Samnites by force. The joy, however, that followed, was by no means so great as the disaster that occurred a few days later, not far from the town of Herdonea. Cneius Fulvius, the consul, was encamped there in the hope of regaining Herdonea, which had revolted from Rome after the defeat at Cannae. His position was neither secure enough due to the nature of the place nor strengthened by guards.

The natural negligence of the general was now increased by the hope that their attachment to the Carthaginians would be shaken when they learned that Hannibal, after the loss of Salapia, had retreated from that neighbourhood into Bruttium. Hannibal ordered his cavalry to ride around as soon as the foot forces were distracted by the battle ahead. He instructed one half of them to attack the enemy's camp, while the other half would strike at their rear, where they were already engaged in combat. The Romans had not been defeated; for after many of them fell in the close contest, and in the engagement with the infantry, they still preserved their ranks and stood their ground. However, the alarm caused by the cavalry on their rear and the enemy's shout, which was heard at the same time from their camp, put the sixth legion to flight. This legion, posted in the second line, was first thrown into confusion by the Numidians. The fifth legion then followed, along with those who were in the van.

Marcellus, undeterred by the magnitude of this disaster, sent a letter to the senate at Rome, detailing the loss of the general and army at Herdonea. He noted that the man who had humbled Hannibal after Cannae's battle, now marched against him, and warned that his present joy would be short-lived. At Rome, the grief occasioned by what had occurred was excessive. The Carthaginians extended their right wing up the hill, with their left wing adjacent to the town.

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'rome__OceanofPDF.com_Emperor_of_Rome_Ruling_the_Ancient_Roman_World_-_Mary_Beard'
output:
Julio-Claudian Dynasty The Julio-Claudian dynasty began with Julius Caesar, who defeated Pompey the Great and became dictator of Rome in 48 BCE. He was assassinated in 44 BCE. Augustus (Octavian), adopted son of Julius Caesar, rose to power after defeating Antonius and Cleopatra in 31 BCE. He became the sole ruler until his death in 14 CE, when he was forced to take his own life following a series of military uprisings.

Macrinus, of equestrian rank, seized power following Caracalla's assassination. He was also reported to have disregarded religious conventions by marrying a Vestal Virgin, one of Rome's most revered priestesses, who was bound to virginity at the risk of death. Ancient accounts of his reign extensively documented the emperor's peculiarities, subversions, and cruelties, including human sacrifice of children.

His crimes and misdemeanors far outdid those of better-known, earlier Roman imperial villains and their supposed villainies: Nero, who played his lyre while Rome burned; Domitian, who relieved boredom by skewering flies with his pen; or Commodus, the anti-hero of Gladiator, who took potshots at the audience in the Colosseum with a bow and arrow. Even Elagabalus' Roman biographer, writing almost two centuries after the emperor's death, conceded that some of the implausible anecdotes he had just recounted were likely inventions, concocted after the emperor's assassination by those who sought to curry favor with his rival and successor on the throne.

History is about more than just the bare facts . On a more modest scale, the tales of Queen Elizabeth II's pampered corgis, reputed to eat their dog food from solid silver bowls, offer us a glimpse into the difference between a 'royal' life and our own. The curious anecdotes about Elagabalus' decision to decorate his summer gardens with ice and snow, or to consume seafood only when far from the sea, suggest that his unusual habits were more than just extravagant self-indulgence.

Julius Caesar, dealing

When Dr Mommsen's work somehow came into my hands years after its initial appearance, and it revived my interest in studies I had long set aside for more professional pursuits. I had little doubt that its merits would have already attracted sufficient attention among Oxford's learned scholars, who might have been inspired to translate it into English. It seemed that despite a strong desire to translate it, and having considered the purpose of translation in multiple quarters, the projects had failed due to various reasons. Mr George Robertson published an excellent translation of the introductory chapters on the early inhabitants of Italy. I wish to acknowledge my obligations to this work as far as it goes.

Many of our translations from German are so literal that they reproduce the very order of the German sentence. This results in translations that are either unintelligible or far from readable for the English reader. In contrast, others deviate so entirely from the original form that they can no longer be considered true translations in the proper sense of the term. This, however, is a characteristic feature of the original. It owes much of its charm to this aspect. Dr Mommsen often employs expressions not found in dictionaries, taking full advantage of the German language's limitless possibilities to coin new terms or combine existing ones. I have not infrequently, in deference to his wishes, used such combinations as "Carthaginian" and "Hellenistic

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'rome__OceanofPDF.com_Daily_Life_in_Ancient_Rome_-_Jerome_Carcopino'
output:
We cannot hope to paint the daily life of our Romani people in its reality unless we first attempt to create a balanced understanding of the setting in which it was lived and shaped, and free ourselves from misconceptions about it. We can satisfactorily study the method in which the Romans lived their lives only after we have mapped out the key elements of the framework that defined their time, both within and outside of which the routine of their daily life would become increasingly obscure. The interior was divided into five naves, each 130 metres long. The central nave reached a width of 25 metres. There were 96 pillars in total. The entire floor was paved with Luna marble, and the roof was adorned with tiles of gilded bronze.

After Trajan's death, his eagle was replaced by a bronze statue of the deceased emperor. It is likely that this statue was destroyed during the turmoil following the invasions. Trajan died unexpectedly in August 117, shortly before he was due to return to Rome. He had recently handed over command of the army he had led against the Parthians to Hadrian. Ammianus Marcellinus has recorded that when the emperor Constantine, accompanied by the Persian king Congiarius, made public distributions of food or money. He stated that he would and could copy the equestrian statue of Trajan.

No prototype has yet been discovered. However, some ancient structures, such as the Aurelian Column in Rome and the columns of Theodosius and Arcadius in Constantinople, may be considered among its posterity, providing examples from antiquity. It sheds light on the idea that drove his ambition, a notion that condemned violence and injustice, leading him to seek any means to find spiritual justification for Roman imperialism. By levelling off the Quirinal, he opened new roads to traffic, as well as added another immense public space in the centre of the city, similar to those created by his predecessors, Caesar, Augustus, the Flavians, and Nerva.

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 37 to the End by Livy'
input: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 37 to the End by Livy
output:
Lucius Cornelius Scipio and Caius Laelius, as consuls, conducted no business in the senate before attending to religious duties. Their ambassadors were pressing because they had a short truce with the Ætolians, but they also received support from Titus Quinctius, who had recently returned from Greece. After several days of debate, it was finally decided that neither peace nor war would be granted or refused.

When this plan, either original or supported by precedents of a record now lost by antiquity, was referred to the senate, it had aroused them by the expectation of a contest. Publius Scipio Africanus said, "If they decreed that province to my brother, Lucius Scipio, I would go along with him as his lieutenant-general." "This proposal was met with universal approval and brought an end to all disputes." The prodigies were repaid, and the Latin festival was celebrated for the second time, as the meat intended for the Laurentians had not been distributed to them. Publius Cornelius Scipio Africanus erected an arch on the Capitol before leaving the city. The arch faced the road leading up to it and featured seven gilded statues and two horses. In front of the arch, he placed two marble cisterns. During that period, forty-three of the principal Ætolians, including Damocritus and his brother, were brought to Rome by two cohorts sent by Manius Acilius. They were imprisoned in the Lautumiæ prison, also known as the quarry.

Lucius Cornelius, the consul, instructed his cohorts to return to the army. The ambassadors who came from Ptolemy and Cleopatra, the king and queen of Egypt, congratulated the Romans because the consul Manius Acilius had driven King Antiochus out of Greece. They advised that the Romans should carry their army into Asia. At the time the consul set out to war, he did so during the celebration of the Apollinarian games on the fifth day before the ides of July. The sky was serene, but the light was obscured in the middle of the day as the moon passed bene

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 01 to 08 by Livy'
input: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome, Books 01 to 08 by Livy
output:
He has not been unmindful of the neatness and perspicacity required to satisfy the English reader. Lucumo came to Rome and assumed the name of Tarquinius. After the death of Ancus, he was elevated to the throne. He increases the senate by adding 100 new senators, defeats the Latins and Sabines, augments the centuries of knights, builds a wall around the city, makes improvements to the common sewers, and is slain by the sons of Ancus after a reign of 38 years. He is succeeded by Servius Tullius.

He institutes the census; closes the lustrum, in which 80,000 citizens are said to have been enrolled. He divides the people into classes and centuries. The Pomœrium is enlarged, and the Quirinal, Viminal, and Esquiline hills are added to the city. Sextus Tarquin was overthrown by his son's stratagem and reduced the city of Gabii. After a 25-year reign, he was deposed and exiled due to the forced rape of Lucretia by his son, Sextus.

When the Trojans, having disembarked there, were driving plunder from the lands, as persons to whom, after their almost immeasurable wandering, nothing was left but their arms and ships, Latinus the king and the Aborigines, who then occupied those places, assembled in arms from the city and country to repel the violence of the new-comers. On this point, the tradition is two-fold. Some say that after being overcome in battle, Latinus made a peace first and then formed an alliance with Aeneas. Others claim that when the armies were drawn up in battle array, before the signals were sounded, Latinus advanced to the front of the troops and invited the leader of the adventurers to a conference. They built a town. Aeneas calls it Lavinium, after the name of his wife.

The Aborigines and Trojans were soon attacked together in war. Nor after that did the Aborigines yield to the Trojans in zeal and fidelity towards their king Æneas; relying therefore on this disposition of the two nations, who were now daily coalescing more and more. Although Etruria was

All members of a gens were descended from a common ancestor, after whom the gens received its name. This body was probably originally composed of all the fathers of families, but in historical times it was limited to 300 members, holding life office and appointed during the regal period by the king. From the death of Marcus Antoninus onwards, it is intended to outline the most important circumstances leading to its decline and fall—a revolution that will be remembered forever and still has a profound impact on the nations of the earth. Military merit, as it is admirably expressed by Tacitus, was, in the strictest sense of the word, imperial virtue. He soon after his election urged Cardinal Perotti to undertake a Latin translation of the existing five books. Casaubon's annotations were published posthumously. Other editions followed. This nine-volume work featured a new Latin translation, largely based on Casaubon, along with a revised text and additional fragments. The lieutenants-general and tribunes, summoned without consultation, gathered around the dejected consul. There was no room for either consultation or remedy. The soldiers, crowding to the general's quarters, demanded succour from their leaders, which it was hardly possible for even the gods themselves to provide. 3 By what road will you reach the enemy? The Samnites, despite their joyful circumstances, were unable to make a decision immediately. It was therefore universally agreed that Herennius Pontius, the general's father, should be consulted by letter.

When these answers, so opposite to each other, like those of an ambiguous oracle, were given, it was decided that despite his son's belief that his father's mind and body had been impaired by age, he should still be consulted due to the general desire of all. He intended to achieve two objectives through his plans. Firstly, he aimed to establish perpetual peace and friendship with a most powerful nation through an extraordinary act of kindness. Secondly, he sought to delay the return of war for many ages, thereby allowing the Roman state sufficient time to recover from the loss of its two armies. "There was a third plan." When his son and the other chiefs went to ask him if "a plan of a middle kind might not be adopted; that they both should be dismissed unhurt, and at the same time, by the right of war, terms imposed upon them as vanquished." "That's indeed a plan of such a nature that neither procures friends nor removes enemies."

Le

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Pre-Colonial Africa/A unique OttomanIslamic perspective_Narrative of Travels in Europe, Asia, and Africa, in the Seventeenth Century, Vol. I'
input: Pre-Colonial Africa/A unique OttomanIslamic perspective_Narrative of Travels in Europe, Asia, and Africa, in the Seventeenth Century, Vol. I
output:
The administration of the mosque remains in the hands of the family. His tutor in reading the Koran was Evliya Muhammad, a learned man, after whom it appears that our traveler was named. He, however, studied in calligraphy, music, grammar, and the Quran. The latter was still under the direction of Evliya Muhiddin, who was then the imperial chaplain (Kunakar Imam). His stay in the imperial palace was very short, for he had been removed from it previously to the Persian expedition, undertaken the same year (1045), against Erivan, when he was enrolled among the Sipahis, with a stipend of forty aspers per annum.

Evliya (the friend of saints), Hafiz (knowing the Quran by heart), and Suyashi (the traveler), are the names by which he styles himself. Although he is more commonly known as Evliya helebib or Efeni, his work is titled Siyyash Nama, or the History of the Traveler. He traveled, as he frequently mentioned, for forty-one years, so that he must have completed his travels by the year 1122. In 1670, when he was sixty-one years old, he devoted the rest of his life to repose and writing about his travels, which extended to all parts of the Ottoman Empire, Europe, Asia, and Africa, except for Tunis, Algiers, and Tripoli, which he never visited. Upon his return, he visited the Prince's Island, and arrived in Constantinople a month after he had left it.

In 1055 (1645), the fleet was fitted out, as was generally rumored, for an expedition against Malta. The Evliya embark on board the ship of the Apuan Pasha, Yusuf Pasha, in the authority of Mózzin-bashi. The expedition, however, had touched at the Morea, surely turned upon a island, where Evliya was present at the rout of the castle of St.

However, upon closer inspection, I noticed that some words are repeated or appear in an incorrect order. Here Ten days after he was again espoused to Eriván, on returning from which he resumed his duties at the usual-house. Note: The original text seems to be a mix of words and phrases

They will allow the officer who obtains permission to go, ensuring his absence on the expedition to retain all the pay and allowances he may be enjoying when leave was granted: they will supply him with all the instruments required, afford him a passage going and returning, and pay the actual expenses of the journey. The navy served under him for ten years despite the ominous presence of profound peace. During the years that elapsed between 1828 and 1838, officers of the Indian Navy journeyed, as the phrase is, with their lives in their hands, through the wildest islands of the East.

It remains, however, for the latter offeror to complete the work. "While these enlightening operations were in progress, there were others of this profession, no less distinguishable, employed on similar investigations." Burnes's companion in his Lahore Afghan mission is a page of history which may not be open to us again in our own times. While in Lieut. Burnes was in Lahore, he had an Afghan companion who shared with him the stories and legends of his people.

The author of these pages, after his return from El Hajaz to Bombay, conceived the idea of reviving the Somali Expedition. He proposed to start in the spring of 1854, accompanied by two officers, to penetrate via Harar and Gananah to Zanzibar.

The most painful events have modified the original plan. Deeply his friends lament him for many reasons: he was a universal favourite, leaving in the social irrelevance a void never to be filled up, and they mourn more that Fate has not granted him the time, as it has given him the will and power to leave a deeper and more enduring mark upon the iron tablets of Fame. He therefore applies to the Bombay Government for the assistance of Lieutenant.

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Pre-Colonial Africa/East Africa (Swahili civilization, Zanzibar, and Somalia)_Zanzibar city, island, and coast (By Richard Francis Burton)'
input: Pre-Colonial Africa/East Africa (Swahili civilization, Zanzibar, and Somalia)_Zanzibar city, island, and coast (By Richard Francis Burton)
output:
Please see the transcriber's note at the end of this text for details regarding the handling of any textual issues encountered during its preparation. Africa, East and West—'Zanzibar' Explained—Menuthias—Position and Formation—The East African Urrent—Navigation—Aspect of the Island. II. A History of Zanzibar.—The Career of the Late 'Imam,' Sayyid Saïd.

May these three aients be typical of the fate of my East African Expedition, which, so long the victim of uncontrollable unrest, appears now, after many weary years, likely to emerge from the shadows that have overtaken it, and to occupy the position I ever desired to see it occupy.

The best authorities are still those who appeared about a quarter of a century ago, always excepting, however, the four magnificent volumes, Baron von Erckens Reisen in Ost-Afrika, in eleven years 1859-1861, which I first saw at Jerusalem: there too I had the pleasure of making acquaintance with Otto Kersten, who accompanied the unfortunate traveler during the earlier portion of his peregrinations, and who has so ably and efficiently performed his part as editor. A certain publisher has expressed his intention of introducing a resume of this fine work in English to the British public. I should have saved myself the trouble of writing these volumes: The Reisen, however, in their original form are hardly likely to become popular.

The result of a skirmish with the Somali of Berberah (April 19, 1855) was, in my absence, a visit 'on sick leave' to England. The campaign, however, was far too advanced; all appointments at headquarters had been filled up; and new omens, such as I was, could only look to the 'Bashi Buzuks,' or to the 'Turkish contingent.' My house was really made. M We had long suspected that General Beatson had refused him a lucrative offer; how we awoke one fine morning to find ourselves in a famous state of siege and blockade, with Turkish muskets on the land side and British armories on the waterfront. We were far more sinne

May these three aients be typical of the fate of my East African Expedition, which, so long the victim of uncontrollable unrest, appears now, after many weary years, likely to emerge from the shadows that have overtaken it, and to occupy the position I ever desired to see it occupy.

The best authorities are still those who appeared about a quarter of a century ago, always excepting, however, the four magnificent volumes, Baron von Erckens Reisen in Ost-Afrika, in eleven years 1859-1861, which I first saw at Jerusalem: there too I had the pleasure of making acquaintance with Otto Kersten, who accompanied the unfortunate traveler during the earlier portion of his peregrinations, and who has so ably and efficiently performed his part as editor. A certain publisher has expressed his intention of introducing a resume of this fine work in English to the British public. I should have saved myself the trouble of writing these volumes: The Reisen, however, in their original form are hardly likely to become popular.

The result of a skirmish with the Somali of Berberah (April 19, 1855) was, in my absence, a visit 'on sick leave' to England. The campaign, however, was far too advanced; all appointments at headquarters had been filled up; and new omens, such as I was, could only look to the 'Bashi Buzuks,' or to the 'Turkish contingent.' It is our duty to consider the possibility that this work may be attributed to the famous navigator Magellan. It must have been through him that it found its way to Charles V's court: there are several reasons for this supposition, and some difficulties in the way of it. I will, however, follow Sir

Ramusio's edition of Barbosa's narrative states that the writing of it was finished in 1516. However, it does not mention any events or dates later than the year 1514. There is reason to suspect that Ramusio obtained his copy from the same source as the Barcelona manuscript, because the name of the precious stone zircon is spelled differently, Giagonza, Jagonza, and Gegonza, and this difference in orthography occurs in the same places in the Spanish manuscript as it does in Ramusio's.

It would be natural to follow Ramusio in attributing the work to him; however, it is not easy to understand how Barbosa, who was in the Indian Ocean at the time, should have encountered two nations at sea in 1508 and 1509, which he relates as one only. The Portuguese were beaten in the first engagement and victorious in the second. It is also difficult to imagine that one person visited all the places described in this volume, even in the space of sixteen years, at a time when travelling was slower than at present: and the observations on the manners and customs show a more intimate knowledge than what would be acquired by touching at a port for a few days only. This work is that of ordinary ability; it shows great power of observation and also the possession by the writer of great opportunities for inquiry into the manners and habits of different countries.

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Pre-Colonial Africa/Malik of the Middle Lakes and Southern Africa (Inland Tribes and Kingdoms)_Missionary Travels and Researches in South Africa by David Livingstone'
input: Pre-Colonial Africa/Malik of the Middle Lakes and Southern Africa (Inland Tribes and Kingdoms)_Missionary Travels and Researches in South Africa by David Livingstone
output:
He was sent to try to meet both the material and spiritual needs of the people he visited, while also promoting trade and attempting to end slavery. He became the first European to cross the continent of Africa, a story that is related in this book. There is no coherent narrative or semantically meaningful content to extract and correct. This work is affectionately offered as a token of gratitude for the kind interest he has always taken in the author's pursuits and welfare. To express admiration of his eminent scientific attainments, nowhere more strongly evinced than by the striking hypothesis respecting the physical formation of the African continent, published in his Presidential Address to the Royal Geographical Society in 1852, verified three years after by the author of these travels. When honored with a special meeting by the Royal Geographical Society a few days after my arrival in London in December last, Sir Roderick Murchison, the President, invited me to give the world a narrative of my travels; and at a similar meeting of the directors of the London Missionary Society I publicly stated my intention of sending a book to the press, instead of making many of those public appearances which were urged upon me.

I have not entered into the early labors, trials, and successes of the missionaries who preceded me in the Bechuanaland country, because that had been done by the more able pen of my father-in-law, Rev. Robert Moffat, of Kuruman, who has been a energetic actor in the scene for over forty years. The beauty of the country—More spending the day—The people and the falls of Gonye—a Makololo foray—a season prevented, and aptives delivered up—Politeness and liberality of the people—the rains—the present of oxen—the fugitive Barotse—Sekobinyane's misgovernment—Bee-eaters and other birds—Fresh-water sponges—Currents—Death from a lion's bite at Libonta—Kindness—Arrangements for spending the night during the journey—Looking and washing—Abundance of animal life—

The expedition led by Speke reached the most southerly point of the lake and named it. . During my sojourn in the interior of Africa, I have endeavored to record all that appears to me most important and interesting among the events and scenes that are under my notice.

But one thing must be remembered: whilst the people of Europe and Asia were blessed by God through the medium of His prophets, and obtained divine laws to regulate their ways and keep them in mind of Him who made them, the Africans were excluded from this dispensation, and consequently had no idea of an overruling Providence or a future state; they therefore trusted only to luck and self-preservation in this world. Whatever the case may be against them for being too varied or too destitute of fellow-feeling, we should rather reflect on ourselves, who have been so much better favored, yet neglected to teach them, than on those who, whilst they are sinning, know not what they are doing. However, Africa is generally uniform in shape; Afria is not.

"Slaves, goods, goats, fowl, brass wire, or beasts, are the usual things given for this species of work." The marriage-knot is not irretrievably tied; for if the wife finds a fault in her husband, she can return to her father by forfeiting her dowry. Meanwhile, the husband, if he objects to his wife, loses half of his sense of her home again, which is considered fair, because as soon as a son-in-law article her future value would be diminished by half. This system must be observed, polygamy is a source of wealth, since a man's means are measured by the number of his progeny. However, it has other advantages besides the offspring, for women work more than men both in and out of doors; additionally, the sons work for the household until they marry, and in after life take care of their parents in the same way as in the first instance when the parents took care of them.

instruction: Summarize the content from 'Pre-Colonial Africa/Malik of the Middle Lakes and Southern Africa (Inland Tribes and Kingdoms)_The Discovery of the Source of the Nile (By John Hanning Speke)'
input: Pre-Colonial Africa/Malik of the Middle Lakes and Southern Africa (Inland Tribes and Kingdoms)_The Discovery of the Source of the Nile (By John Hanning Speke)
output:
The expedition led by Speke reached the most southerly point of the lake and named it. . During my sojourn in the interior of Africa, I have endeavored to record all that appears to me most important and interesting among the events and scenes that are under my notice.

But one thing must be remembered: whilst the people of Europe and Asia were blessed by God through the medium of His prophets, and obtained divine laws to regulate their ways and keep them in mind of Him who made them, the Africans were excluded from this dispensation, and consequently had no idea of an overruling Providence or a future state; they therefore trusted only to luck and self-preservation in this world. Whatever the case may be against them for being too varied or too destitute of fellow-feeling, we should rather reflect on ourselves, who have been so much better favored, yet neglected to teach them, than on those who, whilst they are sinning, know not what they are doing. However, Africa is generally uniform in shape; Afria is not.

His account of himself is that at the age of fourteen, he accompanied his father to Timbuktu, from which town, after a residence of three years, he proceeded to Hausa; and after residing there for two years, he returned to Timbuktu, where he stayed for seven years, and then came back to Tetuan. The cactus (opuntia) is imported from Spain, although the tree that nourishes the cactus fly (ohineal) abounds in many provinces of Western Barbary, particularly in the province of Susa.

The very favourable reception that my account of Maroo met with from the British public; the many things therein stated, which are daily gaining confirmation, although they were doubted at the period of their publication, have contributed in no small degree, to the provision of the following sheets, in which I anxiously declare, that truth has been my guide; I have never sacrificed it to ambition, vanity, avarice, or any other passion. In ten years' time, I anticipate that Fez will be changed to Fas, and Morocco to Maroo, for this plain and uncontroversial reason: because they are spelled as such in the original language of the countries, of which they are the chief cities.

We should be careful not to copy the orthography of Oriental or African names from French, which has too often been one, although their pronunciation of European letters is very similar to our own. Besides, such was the hatred of the Horde of the Welá Slimán and their more of warfare, that after having received the sanction of the British Government for my proceedings, and being authorised by them to carry out the objects of the mission as at first proposed, I would surely venture to associate myself again with such a lawless set of people. The road to the west became open, and I took leave of the sheikh on the 19th of November, in a private arena, with only the vizier present. Matters in Bórnu have changed significantly during my absence in the west. In consequence of the temporary interregnum of the usurper 'Ab-e-Rahman and the overthrow of the vizier, the state of affairs there has become less settled.

My dear servant, among those who stand next to Muhammad in my esteem is 'Abd Allahi, or rather, as his name is pronounced in this country, 'Abd Allahi, a young Shawi from Khotan, whom I had taken into my service on my journey to Bukhara. He was a novice in such situations and had never eaten with Europeans before, which caused me great concern, especially since he was laid up with smallpox for forty days during my stay in that country.

He was a young man of very pleasing manners and straight-forward character. As a good and pious Muslim, he formed a useful link between myself and the Mahommedans; but he was sometimes extremely whimsical, and after having written out his contract for my whole journey to the west and back, I had the greatest trouble in making him adhere to his own stipulations. . Then came Mohammed ben Ahmed, the fellow of whom I have already spoken on my journey to Kánem. Although a person of very indifferent abilities, he was at the same time very self-assured about his Islam, and was valued by me for his honesty. He, on the other hand, having been left by his countrymen and non-believers in a very destitute situation, became attached to myself.

output:
Poor Roo. "No wonder," said Babbalanja, "that the peak is inaccessible to man." How can you hope to breathe in such an atmosphere, unsuited for human lungs? True, my lord," said Babbalanja. "And Bardianna asserts that the plain alone was intended for man; who should be content to dwell under the shade of its groves, though the roots of the earth descended into darkness." The prophet himself was the first to journey there. He then departed for the heavens. Now, excepting this same peak, Maramma is all rolling hills and valleys, like the sea after a storm; which then seems not to move, but stands still, poised on its mountains.

The landscape of Maramma lacks the cheerfulness of meadows. This is due to the shadow of Ofo and the presence of solemn groves where the Morais and temples lie buried. The island is extremely fertile, but its inhabitants believe it would be wrong to cultivate breadfruit. "And thus, my lord," said Babbalanja, "while others attend to their daily concerns, the Islanders pay no heed to tomorrow; and the vast expanse of Maramma stretches out before them as a fertile wasteland." CHAPTER II "It doesn't look like the lost one is here." We finally arrived at a small cove near a valley, which Mohi named Uma. It was there that we brought our canoes to rest.

Nearby, however, were many miserable hovels, with squalid inmates. But the old man's retreat was exceedingly comfortable, especially abounding in mats for lounging; his rafters were bowed down by calabashes of good cheer. During the repast that ensued, blind Pani freely partook and enlarged upon the merit of abstinence, declaring that a thatched roof and a cocoanut tree comprised all that was necessary for the temporal welfare of a Mardian.

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'rome_civilization_The_Satires_of_Juvenal'
output:
Many fine specimens of men and women have been produced without knowledge of these laws. However, in nearly every case, it has been observed that the individual was normal in their habits and temperate in their eating habits, which suggests they were guided by instinct. Giving nature the right material with which to work can lead to better results. By following the lines of least resistance, we can achieve more effective outcomes. The adult requires fewer quantities of the heavier starchy foods, as the body's structural framework has already been established.

We should not consume foods with high caloric or heating values during periods of intense sunlight. This is because the sun's rays are already providing us with warmth, and adding fuel to the situation by building a fire would be unnecessary and potentially hazardous. If we're not going to experience any weather, it's sensible to create a warm environment by consuming high-calorie foods. We should choose and proportion our food based on the type of work we do. Eating is a way to generate energy, while working is a way to expend it. We should strive for balance between the two.

It often occurs that the most balanced menu causes violent physical disturbances, which the inexperienced may view as unfavorable symptoms. However, in most cases, this is simply an adjustment period, comparable to what happens when the body is abruptly deprived of narcotics and stimulants following regular use. The natural world operates with a balance that preserves life. We will first consider the diet and the conduct of the mother during pregnancy and before it.

Preparation for motherhood is one of a woman's most sacred duties. It involves not only her own happiness and health but also has a profound impact on the mental and physical well-being of her child, shaping their entire life. If a new translation of Herodotus does not justify itself, then it is unlikely to be justified in this preface. The question of whether such a translation is needed can be left for consideration elsewhere. As it seemed that even after Stein's recollation of the Medicean manuscript. There were doubts felt by some scholars about the true reading in certain places of this manuscript. The translation of the Bible into the vernacular language has been a subject of great interest and debate among scholars for centuries. It is generally acknowledged that the most important aspect of this process is to examine it closely in all passages where questions about translation arise, particularly in nearly 500 places throughout the text; and the results, when they are worth noting, are recorded in the notes. No consistent system has been adopted, and the result will therefore be open to criticism in many details. The aim has been to avoid altering the form of those names that are fairly established in the English language of literature, while preserving their traditional forms. On the other hand, it is unnecessary to look to Latin for the orthography of names that are not so established, as Greek provides a more suitable alternative.

output:
After that, I took upon myself the task of translating this present book, containing The Metamorphosis of Lucius Apuleius. Inspired by the delightful pastime and engaging matter within, I decided to undertake the challenge. Upon reflection, I considered who would be best suited to offer such a pleasant and worthy work, as devised by the author. By Castor and Pollux, transformed into a heavenly sign known as Gemini, it is indicated that virtuous and godly individuals will be rewarded after death with eternal bliss.

Lucius Apuleius was an African born in Madaura, a country once inhabited by the Romans. Under the jurisdiction of Syphax, he claimed to be half Numidian and half Getulian, a distinction also recognized by Sidonius, who referred to him as the Platonian Madaurence. His father, Theseus, had held all offices of dignity in their country with great honor. Soon after, at Athens (where in times past the well of all doctrine flourished), he tasted many of the cups of the muses. He learned poetry, geometry, music, logic, and the universal knowledge of philosophy. He studied not in vain the nine Muses, that is to say, the nine noble and royal disciplines. Immediately after he went to Rome, he studied the Latin tongue with great labour and continuous study. Through this effort, he achieved remarkable eloquence and was widely recognized as an exceptionally learned individual. This distinction earned him the nickname Polyhistor, meaning someone who knows many things.

And being thus endowed with eloquence, than with singular learning, he wrote many books for those who would come after. Some of which have been lost to time due to negligence, while others still exist, sufficiently demonstrating his wisdom and doctrine, as well as his exceptional virtue a

output:
His work is, in some sense, a collection of anecdotes. It's quite fascinating to read and consult. Combining amusement and information, Suetonius's "Lives of the Caesars" was highly regarded. By the time the printing press was invented in the early 16th century, as many as eighteen editions had already been published, with nearly a hundred more added since then. His translation, however, was quite diffuse and retained most of the inaccuracies found in Clarke's work, upon which it was based. Consequently, considerable care had been taken to correct these errors, with the aim of producing a literal and faithful version. Lives of the most interesting characters are filled with anecdotes and fascinating stories that shed light on the lives of scholars and writers during the time period in question.

Julius Caesar, the divine, lost his father at the age of 16. The following year, nominated for the high-priest office of Jupiter, he rejected Cossutia, who was wealthy despite her family's equestrian status. He had been contracted to her when just a boy. He then married Cornelia, the daughter of Cinna, who was four-time consul, and had by her, shortly afterwards, a daughter named Julia. After changing his place of concealment nearly every night, despite suffering from a quartan ague, he eventually secured his release by bribing the officers who had tracked his footsteps. He then obtained a pardon through the intercession of the vestal virgins and his close relatives, Mamercus Aemilius and Aurelius Cotta.

We are assured that when Sylla finally yielded to the entreaties of his own best friends, persons of distinguished rank, he exclaimed: "Your suit is granted, and you may take him among you. However, know that this man, for whose safety you are so anxious, will one day be the ruin of the party of nobles in defense of which you are allied with me; for in this Caesar, you will find many a Marius." instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'rome_civilization_The_Aeneid_by_Virgil'
output:
The Trojans, after a seven-year voyage, set sail for Italy, but were overtaken by a dreadful storm raised by Aeolus at Juno's request. Venus, by device, instills in Dido a passion for him. After some conversation, she expresses her desire to hear about his adventures since the siege of Troy, which will be the subject of the next two books. Now scarce was the Trojan fleet, with sails and oars, Had left behind the fair Sicilian shores. Entering with cheerful shouts the wat'ry reign, And plowing frothy furrows in the main; When, laboring still with endless discontent, The Queen of Heaven did thus her fury vent: "Then am I vanquished."

These airy kingdoms, and this wide command, Are all the gifts of your bounteous hand: Yours is my sovereign's grace; As your guest, I sit with gods at their celestial feast. You can raise tempests at your pleasure or subdue them; You can dispose of empire, which I hold from you. "He hurled his trembling spear against the mountain's side." His power confined to hollow caverns, there let him reign, The jailer of the wind, with hoarse commands his breathing subjects call, And boast and bluster in his empty hall. He spoke, and with his words, the sea was calm, the darkness lifted, and the day was restored. He stood; and while they fed below him securely, he took the quiver and the trusty bow that Achates had used to bear: first, he laid down the leaders, and then the common people pierced through. He did not cease firing his arrows until seven mighty bodies lay stained with their blood on the shaded plain.

Thus while he dealt it round, the pious chief with cheerful words allayed the common grief: "Endure, and conquer! What greater ills can you bear? You promised once a divine progeny of Romans, rising from the Trojan line, in after times to hold the world in awe, and give the law to land and ocean."

And lest new fears disturb your happy state, know that I have searched the ancient records of fate. Your son, whose time is near, will wage su

I noticed that over time, the amount of time spent on each operation increased. I effected my transcripts in this manner. The work required approximately 195 hours, which was spread over nearly five months. This was not a significant undertaking. If the authorities at the Registrar-General's office were to give their attention to it, they would have abundant data on which to form calculations as to the probable cost of the undertaking. I think that setting aside printing as an after consideration, alphabetical transcripts of all parochial registers in the kingdom could be obtained and deposited in that office at no insurmountable expense. If the cost appears too heavy, the work could be accomplished over a given number of years, say ten or twenty.

On this journey, the intoxicating beverage made from the maguey, known as mead among the Aztecs, and pulque among the Spanish, was invented by a woman whose name was Mayauel. Furthermore, the invention is loosely related to the history of the Huastecs.

You hate this suburb; learn out, go away. If you have never taken a long sea voyage, then the eternities and hardships of many months' travel in a foreign land (the language of whose inhabitants you have been trying to grasp for weeks, struggling with unintelligible grammars) loom ominously in your mind. There is something positively terrifying about standing on the edge of a shipwreck (as all well-known liners insist on being called) on an autumn afternoon. You see early hair on the eyeliner of nitrous oxide and you feel that perhaps after all you've borne the weight.

In the enthusiasm of the moment, we all agree, even those of us who have suffered from the voyage (and they were few) that it was worth coming six thousand miles to see such a sight; and we were all the better for ourselves and our luck because our good skipper, who had sailed to Vera Cruz off and on for a quarter of a century, could it be only one in ten times that the great volcano, Oneensen, exposed its marvelous beauties so well. For example, 720 AH corresponds to the years 1320-21 CE.

Shortly after 1000, the empire collapsed, and Al-Andalus was contested by crusading Christians seeking to "reconquer" Spain, while puritanical Berbers persecuted Christians and Jews. It's a perspective that evokes nostalgia and moralizing, making it appealing despite its melodramatic oversimplifications. Eventually, the term "Morisco" simply came to mean "Muslim" in Castilian Spanish, although in English, the word took on the additional, racially charged baggage of the Elizabethan-era "Blackamoor"—the dark-skinned, African outlaw.

This view has great appeal due to its simplicity and self-evident nature, making it a popular argument among pundits and demagogues across the political spectrum. Ordinary Muslims might serve as mujahidun, stationed in fortress-monasteries that dotted the frontier zone. In contrast, Christian Spain saw raiding become so much a way of life that historians have characterized it as "a society organized for war." "People are far too complex to be reduced to living caricatures of their religious ideologies." In a society formally divided by religion, one's rivals and competitors - whether in personal, political, or financial matters - were typically members of the same faith community. instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Islamic_history /Ottoman_Empire__OceanofPDF.com_Fall_of_the_Ottomans___The_Great_War_in_th_-_Eugene_Rogan'
output:
McDonald and Beveridge said their final farewells to friends and family on May 17, 1915, as their battalion set off for war. As they drew into the island's port of Moudros on May 29—one month after the initial Gallipoli landings—they passed a vast armada of warships and transports lying at anchor.

The magazine described the two young men as the best of friends. They worked together, lived together in rooms, and enlisted together. In their death, they were not divided. Both were young men of sterling character. The obituary concluded that they were well worthy of the positions they held. The magazine expressed sympathy for the bereaved parents of the two boys. One year after the death of their only son, the MacDonalds took the extraordinary step of leaving wartime Scotland to emigrate to the United States. In July 1916, during a pause in German U-boat attacks on Atlantic shipping, they boarded the poignantly named SS Cameronia with two of their daughters, headed for New York City.

The personal connections we make with historical events can explain why the First World War continues to fascinate people a century after it ended. The sheer scale of the mobilization and the carnage left few families unscathed in those countries caught up in the conflict. I came to learn about my great-uncle's history while preparing for a trip to Gallipoli. My great-uncle's unit had suffered 1,400 casualties, half its total strength. Meanwhile, British losses overall reached 3,800, with as many as 14,000 Ottomans falling dead or wounded at Gully Ravine.

We now have a comprehensive understanding of the Allied side of the trenches after a century of research. While there are dozens of diaries and memoirs published in Turkey and the Arab world, few Western historians possess the language skills to read them, and only a fraction of published primary sources are available in translation. Several Turkish and Western scholars have gained access to this collection and are beginning to publish

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Islamic_history /Ottoman_Empire__OceanofPDF.com_Osmans_Dream_-_Caroline_Finkel'
output:
I'm happy to help you with your request, but it seems like you didn't provide any text for me to correct. As the Ottoman period fades from memory, it is now subject to closer examination. Although Turks have traditionally been taught to view themselves as heirs of a storied past that cannot be questioned, this perspective is shifting. The conceit behind the title of my book is rooted in a dream attributed to the first sultan, Osman, which was believed to foretell the emergence and expansion of the empire. This narrative is one that I aim to recount.

I make no claims to completeness, as that would have been impossible to achieve. I have therefore, since they are easy to locate, chosen to use modern place-names as given in The Times Comprehensive Atlas of the World (Millennium Edition, 2000), except in the case of some Ottoman provinces for which using a modern name would be anachronistic. In these cases, I opted for their traditional names, rather than introducing anachronisms or idiosyncrasies that might compromise the historical accuracy of the text. The administrative centre of each province is usually known.

It is clear from contemporary sources that some of these nicknames were bestowed upon individuals while they were still alive, but others were awarded posthumously. An example of the latter is Sultan Süleyman I's soubriquet "Lawgiver," which did not enter common usage until after his death.

Constantine the Great, upon coming to power in 324 CE, founded his new imperial capital, Constantinople, on the Bosporus. Islam arrived in predominantly Christian Anatolia with the Seljuk Turks. Individuals of Turcoman stock had embraced Islam from the ninth century when they came into contact with the Muslim dynasties of the Arab heartlands, often as mercenaries. Mass conversion of Turks in Central Asia was only about a century old. The Mongol Ilkhanid dynasty exerted direct administration during the last quarter of the 13th century; however, their control in Anatolia

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Islamic_history /Ottoman_Empire__OceanofPDF.com_The_imperial_harem_-_Leslie_P_Peirce'
output:
Women should not be involved in matters of government and sovereignty. "While other warnings were directed towards the general population, this proclamation was specifically targeted at the sultan and the royal family, whose senior women had gained a remarkable level of political power in recent years." From the early years of Süleyman the Magnificent's reign, which began in 1520, to the mid-17th century, high-ranking women within the Ottoman dynasty held significant political influence and public prominence, surpassing any previous or subsequent era.

They played a central role in the public culture of sovereignty, encompassing public rituals of imperial legitimization and royal patronage of monumental building and artistic production. PREFACE If the prominence of the imperial harem is a notable feature of the 16th and 17th centuries, then it is also one of the most misunderstood aspects of this period. Recently, new work on this period in Ottoman history has challenged the very notion of post-Süleymanic decline, bringing about a long-overdue reevaluation of developments in the late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries. Even some of these recent works continue to repeat the unexamined theme of the dynasty's dissipation. Women's influence on government conduct increased significantly due to changes in the royal family's social standing and power dynamics.

Because the political culture of the Ottoman state was formed in the context of its subjects' thoughts on society and power, examining the roles played by the various members of the dynasty can provide insight into the views ordinary people held about the proper composition of the family and relationships between the sexes. It is worth noting that while this pattern of control by the junior generation over the senior was dominant in the dynasty for most of the period under study, it appears not to have been as pronounced in earlier stages of Ottoman dynastic history.

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Islamic_history /Islamic_Civilizations_(General)__OceanofPDF.com_The_Great_Arab_Conquests_-_Hugh_Kennedy'
output:
I cannot rewrite historical documents that contain hate speech. The Arabs inhabited the Arabian Peninsula and the Syrian Desert and its surrounding areas. As the conquest progressed, more and more people acquired Arabic as a native language, despite having no 'Arab' ancestry. In many areas where assimilation between conquerors and the conquered advanced most rapidly, the differences between Arabs and non-Arabs had become very blurred by the end of the first Islamic century. Prior to the arrival of Islam, a significant number of Arabs had converted to Christianity, particularly in regions bordering the Syrian desert that lay adjacent to Byzantine territory. Some of them retained their Christian faith after the conquests. Their status became a problem for Muslim jurists in the eighth century: Should they be considered subject people, required to pay the hated poll tax, or treated as Muslim Arabs? The Arab conquerors always demanded payments in cash from the people they conquered. In later centuries, this public taxation was divided by Islamic law into two distinct categories: the khara'j (land tax) and the jizya (poll tax), which were paid only by non-Muslims. During the conquests, the terms became increasingly ambiguous, and jizya referred to any form of taxation or tribute.

Men were named after their fathers, thus Ibn was often abbreviated to 'Ibn'. We also find men called Ibn Abu Fula'n, 'son of the father of so and so'. Women were known as Bint Fula'n, 'daughter of so and so', or, more commonly, as Umm Fula'n, 'mother of so and so'. instruction: What does 'Islamic_history /Islamic_Civilizations_(General)__OceanofPDF.com_The_Great_Arab_Conquests_-_Hugh_Kennedy' say about this topic?
output:
I cannot rewrite historical documents that contain hate speech. The Arabs inhabited the Arabian Peninsula and the Syrian Desert and its surrounding areas. As the conquest progressed, more and more people acquired Arabic as a native language, despite having no 'Arab' ancestry. In many areas where assimilation between conquerors and the conquered advanced most rapidly, the differences between Arabs and non-Arabs had become very blurred by the end of the first Islamic century. Prior to the arrival of Islam, a significant number of Arabs had converted to Christianity, particularly in regions bordering the Syrian desert that lay adjacent to Byzantine territory. Some of them retained their Christian faith after the conquests. Their status became a problem for Muslim jurists in the eighth century: Should they be considered subject people, required to pay the hated poll tax, or treated as Muslim Arabs? The Arab conquerors always demanded payments in cash from the people they conquered. In later centuries, this public taxation was divided by Islamic law into two distinct categories: the khara'j (land tax) and the jizya (poll tax), which were paid only by non-Muslims. During the conquests, the terms became increasingly ambiguous, and jizya referred to any form of taxation or tribute.

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Islamic_history /Islamic_Civilizations_(General)__OceanofPDF.com_Destiny_Disrupted_-_Tamim_Ansary'
output:
"In the early decades following the birth of the Muslim community, I typically use the Islamic date, which consists of two parts: the number of years since the Hijra, denoted by 'AH' (short for 'After the Hijra')." Note that Instead, I used a more precise and coherent phrase to convey the same information. It's useful to convey a feel for the passage of time since the crucial events of Islam.

Later in time, I switched to the "common era" system, as it is the framework most readers are familiar with—and what's the point of giving a date if it doesn't place an event in context and situate it relative to other events? At the time, however, it didn't shape my thinking, because I read history for fun, and in Farsi there wasn't much to read except dull textbooks. I failed to notice these features as a child because Hillyer told a good story.

When we parted, Toynbee gave me a gift: Hendrick Willem Van Loon's The Story of Mankind. I devoured that book and loved it, which became my framework for understanding the Western narrative of world history. Despite studying pedantic Farsi history texts in school, I only read them to pass tests; they were soon forgotten after that.

Faint echoes of another narrative must have lingered within me. Forty years later, in the fall of 2000, when I worked as a textbook editor in the United States, they resurfaced. Note: I made some minor changes to improve readability and flow, while preserving the original meaning. History's narrative is not a comprehensive timeline of every event, but rather a curated selection of pivotal moments that illustrate the unfolding of the story. This is a common practice in elementary and high school textbook publishing. The purpose of these books is to convey, rather than challenge, society's current understanding of what is true.

A root for this book emerged from a course I taught on the Abbasids, which I held during my two-year tenure as the visiting Alfred Howell Chair in the History Department at the American University of Beirut in 2014-2016. Stephanie Taylor played a crucial role at the production stage, ensuring the book project progressed smoothly with all required supplements. Mary Starkey brought her expertise in copyediting to enhance the text's clarity and readability. Although written in modern English transliteration, the name of the fifth caliph, Harun al-Rashid, should be distinguished from that of the later medieval brief ruler, al-Rashid.

Charles Martel's army defeats an Arab force at the Battle of Poitiers. Meanwhile, he secures a Pyrrhic victory over the Turkic Khanate in Central Asia. During the ninth century, the Abbasid capital, Baghdad, experienced a golden age marked by significant scientific and intellectual advancements. The Companions of Muhammad created a temporary government after his death in 632.

The Umayyads attempted to redefine the concept of "caliph" as "deputy of God" instead of "deputy of the Prophet" during the reign of Abd al-Malik. However, this effort ultimately proved unsuccessful, as the title was soon dropped from currency. The task of communicating both a political and religious meaning for the caliphal leadership was far more successfully accomplished by the Abbasids, following the revolution that brought them to power in 750. In social terms, the Abbasid state brought radical change after the Umayyad period in how it opened up the Islamic empire, transitioning from the "Arab" kingdom of the Umayyads, which had relied on government by an Arab tribal confederacy and discouraged conversion to Islam.

This book does not aim to cover too many topics, unlike general histories which often fail to treat each one adequately. The Victorians have been often criticized for their lack of attention to detail in recording historical events. This book aims to highlight its importance.

The outbreak of war marked a most unpremeditated scramble in the stormy late afternoon of the Victorian era. The Pax Britannia, based on naval power, was a long-lasting afterglow of Trafalgar. However, as early as the 1860s, it is noticeable how little effect the world's greatest naval power had on the startling changes that then took place in Victorian England from 1837 to 1901, particularly on the shores of the Baltic Sea and the Mediterranean, and on the other side of the Atlantic. If, however, one looks for the most enduring and instinctive characterist of the Victorian age, one finds it in its high sense of moral responsibility.

The objective here is simply to supply the reader with the details wherein, during a time when many nations were involved in warfare on the front lines, a small minority of the commonwealth armed forces committed what is considered to be the most heinous of crimes, namely murder, rape, and treason, whose names are recorded in perpetuity by the Commonwealth War Graves Commission (CWGC). During the Second World War, a large proportion of British and Commonwealth soldiers behaved in an honest and exemplary fashion throughout their period of service. There was one who shocked others with his rhymes.

Because of their environment, culture, and experience, some may have seen killing as a simple solution, while others did it for sadistic pleasure. Within its grounds stand two splintered memorials that relate to over 4,000 men and women who, during both the First and Second World Wars, had no known grave or whose burial site could not be preserved. Therefore, these assailants and their victims are often ignored and overlooked.

On Sunday, September 3, 1939, Britain declared war on Germany. Parliament immediately passed the National Service (Armed Forces) Act, imposing conscription upon all males aged between 18 and 41, which helped greatly to increase the number of men on active service during the first year of the war. Description, however, generates a good deal of resentment among the refugees as they began a period of basic preparation for military life that included a regime of physical fitness evaluation, discipline and classroom instruction.

Grave Registration Units provided basic details of the individuals, such as name, service number, rank, regiment, unit and date of death. Every effort was made to identify those whose lives had been lost during the period between 3 September 1939 and 31 December 1947. 00 p m The execution.

Eligibility for Commemoration The policy for eligibility of commemoration by the Working Group (WG) regarding those who were former soldiers

While liberties are taken with some of the gods and their relationships, Zelazny uses homages to classical mythology, such as the protagonist Wakim being dismembered limb by limb in a manner similar to Osiris, and the conflict between Horus and Set mirroring the various contests between the two gods in Egyptian sources. The Egyptian gods' ambiguity in their myths enables Robert Zelazny to creatively reinterpret and modify their representations, remaining faithful to their original essence. Osiris sends his son Horus, while Anubis relies on a man named Wakim, who is later revealed to be the destroyer Set, in addition to being Thoth's father.

Zelazny's primary focus lies in ancient Egyptian religion. He occasionally explores Greek mythology, introducing Typhon as the brother of his Egyptian equivalent, Set. "Roger Zelazny unifies this mythological mix by using binary code, suggesting that all life boils down to the interaction of zero and one. This idea resonates with all mythologies, as they often explore the concept of infinity and the afterlife." Examinations of historical material in various games are becoming an increasingly popular area of academic study. This analysis often focuses primarily on aesthetic representations, but they represent only a small part of the playful experience. Examinations of historical material in games of all kinds have become a more common academic pursuit. However, these analyses often focus primarily on aesthetic and narrative representations, which are just one aspect of the ludic experience. An evocation, however, is about utilizing expectations of a culture (or perhaps more accurately a pseudo-culture); in the context of games, and in contrast to representations, this is less about anchoring media elements to an at least partially historically accurate past, but rather to produce somewhat familiar narrative or ludic effects for consumers of media. Culture in reality is far more complex than it is often portrayed in games.

Few media consumers may not find these issues jarring. When evoking a historical culture, such problems may not be relevant to a creator's intentions: the aim is usually to access people's existing associations and expectations of it. Aesthetics are often seen as particularly important in Egypomania, and its reception in games poses no exception. Other aspects can also be just as significant.

The fact that the more powerful new piece is referred to as the bishop suggests much about the European societies that adopted this particular adaptation. As with all forms of evocation and representation, we cannot assume intentionality when games model arguments about the cultures and systems they represent. Indeed, whether intentional or not, they inevitably convey such information. The power of evocation is a double-edged sword. Cultural elements that are evoked, even in highly fantastical cultures, tend to be shared with and can evoke real cultures or pseudo-cultures to varying degrees. Resources can be either discrete items or continuous variables. Expenditur

Focusing on artists who came after jazz musician Sun Ra, working between the 1970s and the present day, I highlight the ways in which ancient Egypt is remembered and reimagined in Afrofuturism within the Afrodiasporic community of the US. This is particularly readable because Afrofuturist narratives are widely and influentially spread. Available with this essay is a Spotify playlist featuring the music discussed. Afrofuturism in the twenty-first century is expressed in metaphysics, religion, speculative philosophy, and philosophy of science and technology. Jones This article, however, explores Afrofuturism as a form of speculative fiction addressing contemporary concerns relevant to Black African communities and the African diaspora. Afrofuturism often involves rejecting the definition of the past solely based on the traumas of slavery and the transatlantic slave trade. Afrofuturists emphasize that such retellings are necessary. They argue that the way the past is understood is inherently political because it is produced and sustained by hegemonic institutions, including universities and museums. From an Afrofuturist perspective, and as is explored later in this article, the ways in which futures are created and imagined are equally political.

The Afrodiasporic community in California identifies with the Egyptian god Osiris, linking social justice and progress to ancient Egyptian royalty. Janelle Monáe is an example of a prominent feminist Afrofuturist artist. Her work explores themes and ideas that intersect with ancient Egyptian culture. Enslaved people and robots are often viewed as analogous, neither fully human nor fully safe in the eyes of their masters. They are both subjected to forced labor. Some Afrofuturist artists subvert this narrative.

The androids presented by The Egyptian Lover and Erykah Badu embody multiple layers of time, challenging their relationship with servitude. Sun Ra was mentioned by both Om'mas Keith and Taz Arnold in the same interview

They hint not only at the "queerness" perceived in ancient Egyptian culture but also at individuals who encounter and engage with ancient Egypt themselves. Voss explains that in the 19th century, Europeans turned to Egyptology for models of sexual potency, bisexuality, gender ambiguity, and homoeroticism. Cleopatra, renowned for her seductive powers, was the figurehead of a complex web of sexual connotations. Nineteenth-century images of the Egyptian queen, as observed by Lynn Meskell, often focus on "the breast and the phallic snake", combining imagery of femininity and masculinity. I would add that the combination of masculine and feminine aspects about a single body, particularly towards the end of the 19th century during the Victorian era's magical revival, was increasingly interpreted as alchemical.

The vulnerability of wood to moisture and other threats, including termites, has often been overlooked. Fortunately, evidence survives to confirm that wooden sculpture played a significant role in ancient funerary and ritual practices. We should bear in mind, however, that as yet relatively few statues have had their wooden statuary restored. The inscriptions accompanying these scenes rarely refer to the statues depicted. However, the tools shown are a good indication of the material in question. An adze in the hand of a workman suggests that the material is wood, while hammers and mallets typically indicate that they are used for working stone.

Few conclusions can be drawn from this, however, beyond noting that the wooden statues from Beni Hassan tend to have extremely large, painted eyes. During the New Kingdom and later, the main workshops were located in Thebes and Memphis. It will come as no surprise that most provenanced statues from these periods originate from these two locations. As we move on in time, the number of wooden statues in each tomb increases, while their size and quality decrease.

The well-known offering formula "for the ka of" does not appear until the very end of the period. However, it became a standard during the First Intermediate Period and the Middle Kingdom. During the First Intermediate Period and Middle Kingdom, the focus shifted from the tomb owner and his wife to include models of daily life. As the period progresses, the quality of figures and models generally declines, with a few exceptions remaining.

The average size of wooden statues decreased after the 11th Dynasty. Although the range of costumes and wigs on male statues was broader, it remained impossible to identify specific costumes and wigs associated with particular offices. Pair statues are extremely rare throughout history. For instance, only a few examples are known from the second half of the 18th Dynasty.

In addition to the names and iconography of the moon, Derchain discussed various Egyptian moon deities and their role in different contexts. These included the celestial body's use in calendars and its perception as an omen, as well as its significance for the afterlife, particularly as presented in the Pyramid Texts. The author has published a study (Altmann-Wendling 2018) on lunar conceptualizations and lunar knowledge, primarily based on religious sources. This work covers the Ptolemaic and Roman periods, as well as earlier periods and authors from classical antiquity. The moon's luminosity is significantly lower than the sun's, but a full moon can still make the night sky appear brighter.

The Moon was depicted visually in ancient Egyptian rituals from the New Kingdom period onwards. Unlike other natural phenomena, which were often anthropomorphized, the moon was represented as an actual celestial body. This is evident in ritual scenes from this time period, where the moon is shown alongside the moon god Iah, sharing its name. The disk with the wedjat-eye clearly refers to the moon rather than the sun. g Sauneron, 1969: 1-4; Altmann-Wendling, 2018: 610. References to other colors as well as to the "skin" of a celestial body might refer to its cratered surface, its appearance during a lunar eclipse, or its changing appearance depending on its phase and position.

The moon's illumination is comparable to that of the sun during the day and can be used to determine the size of a ruler's territory. The rising of the moon is a particularly impressive sight on the horizon, making the Earth's satellite appear larger and closer due to an optical illusion. The moon's appearance in the west was of great significance. The frequent mention of this in Egyptian texts is a recurring theme that warrants closer examination. g The text appears to be incomplete and lacks context. During the Byzantine period and later, the number of surviving temples was further diminished. The walls were dismantled. Limestone blocks were taken to lime kilns, while granite blocks were reused in village construction. Cities and Byzantine/Islamic-period monuments also benefited from this reuse. Mudbrick remains suffered due to the activities of the sebakhin and the deteriorating influence of the wet Delta climate. Pinedjem continued the decoration of the Khonsu temple at Karnak. During his reign, restorations were undertaken at Medinet Habu, as confirmed by the restoration texts.

The tombs were polytaphs, containing multiple burials, and after various changes served as royal caches. A temple dedicated to Khonsu may have been built north of the Amun temple at that time. A temple dedicated to Amun of Ipet was discovered at the end of the 20th century. It was built on the southern part of a tell, modeled after the one in Luxor.

instruction: Summarize the content from 'Egyptian civilization/UEE_article 2'
output:
During the Byzantine period and later, the number of surviving temples was further diminished. The walls were dismantled. Limestone blocks were taken to lime kilns, while granite blocks were reused in village construction. Cities and Byzantine/Islamic-period monuments also benefited from this reuse. Mudbrick remains suffered due to the activities of the sebakhin and the deteriorating influence of the wet Delta climate. Pinedjem continued the decoration of the Khonsu temple at Karnak. During his reign, restorations were undertaken at Medinet Habu, as confirmed by the restoration texts.

The tombs were polytaphs, containing multiple burials, and after various changes served as royal caches. A temple dedicated to Khonsu may have been built north of the Amun temple at that time. Later, it was rebuilt during the 30th Dynasty, as evidenced by the discovery of statues of the god in the form of a baboon and cartouches of Psusennes I. A temple dedicated to Amun of Ipet was discovered at the end of the 20th century. It was built on the southern part of a tell, modeled after the one in Luxor.

instruction: Provide a detailed explanation from 'Egyptian civilization/UEE_article 3'
output:
The moon, with its easily perceivable and regularly changing shape, is virtually ideal as a timekeeping instrument. Its ever-repeating cycle of phases - from invisibility to fullness and back again - vividly illustrates the ancient Egyptian concept of cyclical time and eternity. However, upon closer inspection, the moon's role as a timekeeper has two significant drawbacks: Firstly, its lunar month can vary in length between 29 and 30 days.

It is likely that an Egyptian lunar calendar existed during the Predynastic and Protodynastic periods, which are often referred to as Dynasty 0. As Sirius rises with the sun, it becomes visible in the eastern sky just before dawn, appearing for the first time after being hidden from view in the night sky. They argue that this can be explained in two ways. On one hand, it explains a single year length as recorded on the Palermo stone. : 323) There is nevertheless mutual agreement that a lunar calendar existed prior to the inception of the civil calendar. g Spalinger (2018): 4; Depuydt (2017): 273; Belmonte and Lull (2023): 328.

Both dates are incomplete. One records a Wägy-feast on the third month [day 28], with the season missing. The other mentions an event after the Wägy-feast in the first month of the Axt season, but lacks the day number. The event following the feast likely took place on a day between January 23rd and January 29th. The moon's changing shape held significant importance in the religious and cultic spheres, even after the introduction of the civil calendar. Some lunar events relate to specific phases of the moon, including new moon, first crescent, first quarter, full moon, and last quarter. Others are based on numbers, like the sixth or eighth month, while a few pertain to specific festivals.

The names in the astronomical frieze of the Temple of Edfu appear to follow closely the ones found in the Eighteenth Dynasty tomb of Senenmut at Deir el-Bahri. However, it is more evident how similar the Greek month name
